title: KEI-61 — Explicit deprecation layer: bd deprecate command + environment-change invalidation
description:
## Problem

Two related issues:

**1. No explicit deprecation command.** [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) handles staleness (time-based aging). But when an infrastructure migration makes a discovery actively wrong — not just old — there's no mechanism for an agent to explicitly say "this is no longer valid." The discovery continues surfacing in bd claim injections until the 90-day flag eventually appears.

**2. No environment-change invalidation.** When we migrate off Vultr, switch container runtimes, or upgrade major software versions, discoveries tied to the old environment are invalidated immediately — not in 90 days. Time-based staleness misses this entirely.

## Fix Part 1: bd deprecate command

```bash
bd deprecate KEI-44-cgroup-discovery --reason "Migrated off Vultr, containerization strategy changed"
```

This:

1. Sets `status = 'deprecated'` on the discovery object in Weaviate
2. Sets `deprecated_by = agent_callsign`, `deprecated_at = now()`, `deprecation_reason = text`
3. Deprecated discoveries NEVER appear in bd claim injections
4. Remain in Weaviate permanently — audit trail preserved, never deleted
5. Writes deprecation event to Supabase audit_logs

Tier rules (from [KEI-53](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-53/kei-51-agent-profiles-personalised-bd-ready-queue-with-capability)):

* Tier 1 deprecation: any agent can deprecate without approval
* Tier 2 deprecation: requires peer CONCUR before taking effect
* Tier 3 deprecation: contradicts a ratified decision — requires Dave approval

## Fix Part 2: Environment hash invalidation (extends [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount))

Every discovery has an `environment_hash` (from [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount)). When the current environment changes significantly, the system runs a comparison:

```python
# Runs on any infrastructure event: migration, major upgrade, provider change
def check_environment_invalidations():
    current_hash = compute_environment_hash()
  
    # Find discoveries whose environment doesn't match current
    stale_by_context = weaviate.query(
        "discoveries",
        where={"environment_hash.hash": {"not_equal": current_hash.hash}}
    )
  
    for discovery in stale_by_context:
        # Flag as 'context_changed' — different from 'stale' (age-based)
        inject_context_change_flag(discovery)
```

Context-changed discoveries appear in bd claim injection with:
`"⚠⚠ Environment changed since this was written — likely invalid"`

Different warning from staleness (age). Agents immediately know WHY it's flagged.

## Fix Part 3: Eviction layer summary

Three distinct knowledge states, each handled differently:

| State | Trigger | Visibility | Action required |
| -- | -- | -- | -- |
| Active | Default | Full trust in injections | None |
| Stale | Age > 90 days | ⚠ age warning | Verify or let age |
| Context-changed | Environment hash mismatch | ⚠⚠ context warning | bd verify or bd deprecate |
| Deprecated | bd deprecate command | Never shown in injections | Already actioned |

## Acceptance criteria

* `bd deprecate [discovery_id] --reason "..."` command implemented
* Deprecated discoveries excluded from all bd claim injections immediately
* Deprecation events logged to audit_logs with agent, reason, timestamp
* Environment hash comparison runs on infrastructure change events
* Context-changed flag distinct from staleness flag in bd claim injection
* Tier-based approval for Tier 2/3 deprecations enforced

## Extends: [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) (staleness), [KEI-60](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-60/kei-58-weaviate-persistent-storage-governance-volume-mount) (environment_hash)

## Owner

Aiden — after [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) ships. Same domain.

title: KEI-60 — Embedding model independence: raw text storage + re-embed-corpus.py migration pipeline
description:
## Problem

Weaviate vectors are built using Gemini `gemini-embedding-001`. When Google deprecates this model (which they will — PaLM embeddings, ada-001, ada-002 were all deprecated), the entire Weaviate knowledge base becomes unqueryable overnight. You cannot query a vector space built on Model A using embeddings from Model B. The result is semantic nonsense.

All collective intelligence accumulated by the team is gone — not because the data is lost, but because the vector space is unreadable.

## Fix Part 1: Raw text stored alongside every vector (add to [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) spec)

Every Weaviate document object must store the original sanitised text explicitly as a property, not only as a vector.

```python
weaviate_client.batch.add_object(
    class_name="discoveries",
    properties={
        "raw_text": sanitised_text,       # ← CRITICAL: always store this
        "agent": "max",
        "kei": "KEI-44",
        "finding": "...",
        "tags": ["cognee", "memory"],
        "created_at": timestamp
    },
    vector=gemini_embed(sanitised_text)   # vector can be regenerated from raw_text
)
```

Cost: negligible. A 500-token discovery log is \~400 bytes of text. The entire corpus at 42,000 documents is \~17MB of raw text storage.

## Fix Part 2: [re-embed-corpus.py](<http://re-embed-corpus.py>) — the insurance policy

A tested, documented migration script stored in the Agency_OS repo. Written now, before it is ever needed.

```python
# re-embed-corpus.py
# Use when: embedding model deprecation, model upgrade, model switch
# Runtime: runs overnight against full corpus

def re_embed_corpus(
    old_model: str,
    new_model: str, 
    collections: list[str],
    dry_run: bool = True
):
    """
    1. Reads all objects from Weaviate via raw_text property
    2. Re-embeds using new_model
    3. Overwrites vector column in-place
    4. Verifies: 10 spot-check queries return expected results
    5. Writes audit log: re-embed complete, N objects, model transition
    """
```

The script is stored in:

* `scripts/re_embed_corpus.py` in Agency_OS repo
* Reference stored in Supabase ceo_memory as `ceo:tools:re_embed_corpus`

## Fix Part 3: Environment hash on all discovery objects

The `environment_hash` captures the full execution context at write time, not just software versions:

```json
"environment_hash": {
  "infrastructure": "vultr-sydney-vx1-16gb",
  "container_runtime": "docker-24.0.5",
  "os": "ubuntu-24.04",
  "embedding_model": "gemini/gemini-embedding-001",
  "key_software": {"cognee": "0.7.3", "weaviate": "1.36.0"},
  "hash": "sha256:..."
}
```

When infrastructure or embedding model changes significantly, all discoveries whose `environment_hash` doesn't match current environment are flagged — regardless of age. This complements [KEI-56](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-56/kei-54-resource-governance-weaviate-cgroup-cap-continuous-ram) (time-based staleness) with context-based invalidation.

## Fix Part 4: Embedding model pinning

Pin to a specific model version, never `latest`:

```
gemini-embedding-001  → explicit version, not 'latest'
```

Monitor for deprecation notices. Set a Google Cloud alert on the model version we use.

## Acceptance criteria

* Every Weaviate document has `raw_text` property storing sanitised source text
* `scripts/re_embed_corpus.py` exists, is documented, and has been tested end-to-end (dry_run=True)
* Re-embed test: create 10 test objects, run re-embed with a different model config, verify all 10 are retrievable with correct results
* `environment_hash` present on all discovery objects
* Embedding model version pinned (not 'latest') in configuration

## Updates [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) scope: raw text + environment_hash are required schema properties for all collections

## Owner

Elliot — implement raw_text storage as part of [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) schema design. Write re_embed_corpus.py now, before [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) deployment.
close_reason: KEI-60 merged fb272743 — environment_hash + re_embed_corpus.py + schema docs live on main. Pre-condition for KEI-46 cleared.

title: KEI-59 — Durable indexing queue: Supabase staging buffer prevents dropped webhooks and knowledge loss
description:
## Problem

The capture layer connects webhooks (Linear, Slack, Git) directly to LlamaIndex processing. With 6 agents working in parallel, webhook traffic is bursty. If LlamaIndex is busy processing a large commit or restarts mid-operation, incoming webhooks fire into nothing. A dropped webhook = permanent knowledge loss — no error, no retry, silent gap.

## Fix: Supabase indexing_queue as durable staging buffer

Standard durable queue pattern. Webhooks never touch LlamaIndex directly.

### Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE indexing_queue (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  source TEXT NOT NULL,          -- 'git' | 'slack' | 'linear' | 'ceo_memory' | 'tool_log'
  payload JSONB NOT NULL,        -- raw webhook payload (pre-sanitisation)
  status TEXT DEFAULT 'pending', -- 'pending' | 'processing' | 'done' | 'failed'
  attempts INT DEFAULT 0,
  error TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  indexed_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);

CREATE INDEX idx_indexing_queue_status ON indexing_queue(status, created_at);
```

### Webhook receiver (fast, always returns 200)

```python
@app.post("/webhook/{source}")
async def receive_webhook(source: str, payload: dict):
    # Step 1: Sanitise before storage
    sanitised = sanitise(json.dumps(payload))
  
    # Step 2: Write to queue immediately (< 5ms)
    supabase.table('indexing_queue').insert({
        'source': source,
        'payload': json.loads(sanitised),
        'status': 'pending'
    }).execute()
  
    # Step 3: Return 200 immediately — never make the sender wait
    return {"status": "queued"}
```

### Queue worker (separate systemd service)

```python
# Polls indexing_queue every 10 seconds
# Uses SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED to prevent double-processing
while True:
    rows = supabase.rpc('claim_queue_batch', {'batch_size': 5}).execute()
    for row in rows.data:
        try:
            process_via_llamaindex(row)
            index_to_weaviate(row)
            mark_done(row['id'])
            write_audit_log(row)
        except Exception as e:
            mark_failed(row['id'], str(e))
            if row['attempts'] >= 3:
                alert_ceo(f"Indexing failed after 3 attempts: {row['id']}")
    time.sleep(10)
```

### Retry logic

* Failed rows retry up to 3 times with exponential backoff
* After 3 failures: status = 'failed', alert to #ceo, do not retry
* Stuck 'processing' rows (>10 min): reset to 'pending' for retry

### Audit log requirement

Every successful index writes to audit_logs:

```json
{"event": "indexed", "source": "git", "kei_tags": ["cognee"], "weaviate_id": "...", "tokens": 340, "timestamp": "..."}
```

## Monitoring

* Queue depth > 100 pending → warning to #ceo
* Queue depth > 500 pending → CRITICAL to #ceo (processing not keeping up)
* Any row stuck in 'processing' > 10 min → alert

## Acceptance criteria

* `indexing_queue` table created in Supabase
* Webhook receiver writes to queue and returns 200 within 50ms
* Queue worker running as systemd service, survives restart
* Retry logic tested: simulate LlamaIndex crash mid-processing, verify row retries
* Audit log entry confirmed for every successful Weaviate write
* Queue depth monitoring alerts firing correctly

## BLOCKS: [KEI-48](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-48/kei-46-install-weaviate-on-vultr-sydney-collective-intelligence) (auto-indexing pipeline must use queue-backed architecture)

## Owner

Scout — design and implementation. After [KEI-57](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-57/kei-55-context-injection-ceiling-500-tokens-discovery-log-format-v2) (sanitisation) ships.

title: KEI-58 — Weaviate persistent storage governance: volume mount verification + backup schedule (CRITICAL pre-condition)
description:
## Problem

Weaviate running via Docker without explicit persistent volume mounts loses ALL data on container restart, server reboot, or OOM crash. Given we crashed the server once already today, and OOM crashes are a known risk, the entire collective intelligence knowledge base could be wiped at any moment without this fix.

All 11 KEIs of architecture work is worthless if the data doesn't survive a reboot.

## Fix Part 1: Governance trace before Weaviate starts

Before Weaviate is started for the first time, Elliot must provide:

```bash
cat /home/elliotbot/clawd/docker-compose.yml
# or wherever the Weaviate compose file lives
```

We verify the output confirms:

1. Weaviate data directory explicitly mapped to a path OUTSIDE the container
2. That path is on Vultr's persistent block storage (not tmpfs or container ephemeral)
3. Volume mount confirmed in the governance trace output

No data enters Weaviate until this is confirmed.

## Fix Part 2: Correct docker-compose.yml volume configuration

```yaml
version: '3.8'
services:
  weaviate:
    image: semitechnologies/weaviate:1.36.0
    restart: on-failure
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data:/var/lib/weaviate  # ← CRITICAL: host path
    environment:
      QUERY_DEFAULTS_LIMIT: 25
      AUTHENTICATION_ANONYMOUS_ACCESS_ENABLED: 'true'
      PERSISTENCE_DATA_PATH: '/var/lib/weaviate'
      DEFAULT_VECTORIZER_MODULE: 'none'
      CLUSTER_HOSTNAME: 'node1'
    deploy:
      resources:
        limits:
          memory: 2560M   # ← cgroup cap from KEI-54
```

The `/home/elliotbot/weaviate-data` directory must be created on the host BEFORE starting:

```bash
mkdir -p /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data
```

## Fix Part 3: Backup schedule

Even with persistent volumes, the Vultr block storage itself could fail. Daily backup to Vultr Object Storage (S3-compatible):

```bash
# Daily cron: compress and upload Weaviate data directory
tar -czf /tmp/weaviate-backup-$(date +%Y%m%d).tar.gz /home/elliotbot/weaviate-data
# Upload to Vultr Object Storage bucket
```

Backup retention: 7 days rolling.

## Fix Part 4: Startup verification

On every Weaviate startup, run a health check that confirms:

1. Data directory is accessible and non-empty (after first run)
2. Known test object is retrievable
3. If health check fails: DO NOT proceed — alert to #ceo

## Acceptance criteria

* docker-compose.yml governance trace provided and verified before first run
* `/home/elliotbot/weaviate-data` confirmed on persistent host storage
* Volume mount verified: `docker inspect weaviate` shows correct bind mount
* Startup health check confirms data survives container restart (test: write object, restart container, verify object exists)
* Daily backup cron running to Vultr Object Storage
* Server reboot test: all Weaviate data survives

## BLOCKS: [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) execution (Weaviate cannot be started until this is verified)

## Owner

Atlas — verify and implement concurrent with [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per). These are the same deployment task.

title: KEI-57 — Secret redaction middleware: sanitise all capture sources before embedding (CRITICAL pre-condition)
description:
## Problem

The capture layer indexes raw content from Git commits, Slack messages, Claude Code tool call logs, and ceo_memory. When agents debug issues, they run commands like `cat .env`, query database URIs, or print API keys to stdout. That raw output gets embedded by Gemini and stored permanently in Weaviate. Every agent gains semantic access to production secrets forever.

This is a security incident, not a bug. Must be resolved before any data enters the pipeline.

## Fix: Sanitisation middleware as first step in every capture pipeline

Runs BEFORE LlamaIndex. BEFORE embedding. BEFORE Weaviate. Nothing passes through without being sanitised first.

### Patterns to redact (replace with \[REDACTED\])

```python
SECRET_PATTERNS = [
    # API keys
    r'sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{20,}',           # OpenAI / Anthropic
    r'AIza[0-9A-Za-z\-_]{35}',        # Google API keys
    r'sk-ant-[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]{80,}',    # Anthropic
    r'Bearer [a-zA-Z0-9\-_\.]{20,}',  # Bearer tokens
  
    # JWTs (three base64 segments)
    r'eyJ[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+\.eyJ[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+\.[a-zA-Z0-9\-_]+',
  
    # Database connection strings
    r'(postgres|postgresql|mysql|mongodb|redis)://[^\s]+',
  
    # AWS credentials
    r'AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}',               # AWS access key ID
    r'[a-zA-Z0-9/+]{40}',              # AWS secret (contextual)
  
    # Generic high-entropy strings in .env context
    r'(?i)(password|secret|token|key|passwd)\s*[=:]\s*\S{8,}',
  
    # Common env file patterns
    r'[A-Z_]+(KEY|SECRET|TOKEN|PASSWORD|PASSWD|PWD)\s*=\s*\S+',
  
    # Private keys
    r'-----BEGIN (RSA |EC |OPENSSH )?PRIVATE KEY-----',
]
```

### Implementation

```python
import re

def sanitise(text: str) -> str:
    for pattern in SECRET_PATTERNS:
        text = re.sub(pattern, '[REDACTED]', text)
    return text
```

### Integration points

Must be applied to ALL five capture sources:

1. Git commit content → sanitise before chunking
2. Slack message content → sanitise before indexing
3. Linear KEI content → sanitise before indexing
4. Claude Code tool call logs → sanitise ESPECIALLY this one
5. ceo_memory entries → sanitise before indexing

### Audit logging

Every redaction event logged to Supabase audit_logs:

```json
{"event": "secret_redacted", "source": "tool_call_log", "pattern_matched": "Bearer token", "agent": "max", "kei": "KEI-44", "timestamp": "..."}
```

Allows review of what was redacted without logging the actual secret.

### Test suite required

Before shipping: test corpus of 20 strings containing known secret formats. All must be redacted. Zero false negatives acceptable on explicit patterns.

## Acceptance criteria

* Sanitisation middleware exists as a standalone module: `src/memory/sanitise.py`
* All 5 capture sources pass content through sanitise() before any further processing
* 20-item test corpus passes with zero false negatives
* Redaction events logged to audit_logs
* No raw secret ever reaches LlamaIndex, Gemini embeddings, or Weaviate

## BLOCKS: [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per), [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from), [KEI-48](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-48/kei-46-install-weaviate-on-vultr-sydney-collective-intelligence) (cannot start until this ships)

## Owner

Elliot — build this FIRST before any other collective intelligence KEI.
close_reason: KEI-57 merged sha ce8e6cc3 — secret redaction module + 33 tests + runbook live on main. Pre-condition cleared for KEI-46/47/48.

title: KEI-56 — Discovery staleness governance: context_version field + age flags + bd verify command
description:
## Problem: Stale Verified Paths

"cgroup v2 is the verified path" is true today. Cognee releases a new version next month that handles memory differently. The discovery is now wrong. But it still appears in bd claim injections as a green-lit "verified_path" with no indication it may be outdated.

Agents acting on stale verified paths make wrong decisions confidently — arguably worse than no memory at all.

## Fix: context_version field + staleness flagging

### context_version field (added to discovery log format)

```json
{
  "context_version": {
    "kei": "KEI-44",
    "date": "2026-05-13",
    "software_versions": {
      "cognee": "0.7.3",
      "python": "3.12.2",
      "weaviate": null
    }
  }
}
```

Automatically populated from the system environment at write time. Agent doesn't need to fill this in.

### Staleness flagging in bd claim injection

When a discovery is injected into a context brief, the age is evaluated:

```
0–30 days:   No flag. Full trust.
30–90 days:  "[~X days old]" inline label. Informational only.
90+ days:    "⚠ [X days old — verify still applies]" warning label.
180+ days:   "⚠⚠ [X days old — likely stale, re-verify before use]" strong warning.
```

No auto-deletion ever. The warning is the mechanism. Agents decide whether to act on old knowledge.

### Re-verification path

Any agent can run:
`bd verify [discovery_id]`

This:

1. Opens the discovery for review
2. Agent can CONFIRM (re-timestamps to today), UPDATE (creates new version), or DEPRECATE (marks as superseded)
3. Confirmed/updated discoveries reset their staleness clock
4. Deprecated discoveries remain in Weaviate but never appear in injections

### Software version change detection

If the `software_versions` in a discovery doesn't match current installed versions: always flag as potentially stale regardless of age.
e.g., Cognee 0.7.3 discovery + current Cognee 0.9.1 = "⚠ Written for Cognee 0.7.3, current is 0.9.1 — verify"

## Integration with [KEI-53](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-53/kei-51-agent-profiles-personalised-bd-ready-queue-with-capability) (validation tiers)

Re-verification feeds back into the validation tier system. A Tier 2 discovery that has been re-verified resets its staging clock. A Tier 2 discovery that has never been re-verified after 90 days is downgraded to "stale — requires re-verification."

## Acceptance criteria

* context_version auto-populated at discovery write time (no agent action)
* Staleness flags appear correctly in bd claim injection at 30/90/180 day thresholds
* `bd verify [id]` command implemented with CONFIRM/UPDATE/DEPRECATE options
* Software version mismatch detection working (test: change recorded Cognee version)
* Deprecated discoveries excluded from all bd claim injections

## Owner

Scout — research into implementation patterns, then build. After [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per)+47 ship.

title: KEI-55 — Context injection ceiling (500 tokens) + discovery log format v2 (failed_path/verified_path)
description:
## Problems addressed

### 1\. Context Window Token Bloat

Unbounded bd claim injection inflates every API call. At 6 agents × multiple claims per day × uncapped tokens = significant Anthropic API cost increase and diluted agent attention.

### 2\. Discovery Log Negative Constraint

"do_not_repeat" is a negative instruction. LLMs handle positive instructions better. The format should present both what failed and what works.

## Fix 1: 500-token hard ceiling on bd claim injection

LlamaIndex CitationQueryEngine configured with `max_tokens=500` for the auto-injection path specifically.

Format enforced:

```
[CONTEXT BRIEF — KEI-XX] (max 500 tokens / ~375 words)

Prior discoveries (max 2): [cited]
Related decisions (max 2): [cited]
Relevant files (max 3): [path + one line]
Watch out for (max 2): [verified failed paths only]
```

If LlamaIndex retrieves more: truncate by relevance score. Most relevant content wins.

Exception: Agent can explicitly query Weaviate directly mid-task with no token limit. The ceiling applies ONLY to the automatic bd claim injection. Agent choice is uncapped.

## Fix 2: Updated Discovery Log format (5 fields)

```json
{
  "agent": "max",
  "kei": "KEI-44",
  "context": "Cognee memory cap implementation",
  "finding": "Cognee graph DB pre-allocates ~4GB virtual memory at startup. ulimit measures virtual — fires before real work. cgroup v2 memory.max measures RSS only.",
  "failed_path": "ulimit -v — blocks virtual memory reservation, fires before any real processing begins",
  "verified_path": "cgroup v2 memory.max with systemd MemoryMax= directive — measures RSS only, allows pre-allocation",
  "tags": ["cognee", "memory", "cgroup", "ulimit", "oom"]
}
```

Changes from original design:

* "discovery" → "finding" (neutral, factual framing)
* "do_not_repeat" → split into "failed_path" + "verified_path"
* Both positive and negative paths explicit
* Agent reading this knows exactly what to use AND exactly what to avoid

## Update required: [KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) description

[KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) (Structured discovery log) must be updated to use this 5-field format. The `bd discover` command must prompt for all 5 fields.

## Update required: [KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) description

[KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) (bd claim context injection) must enforce the 500-token ceiling at the LlamaIndex layer.

## Acceptance criteria

* bd claim injection never exceeds 500 tokens (verified by token counter in logs)
* Discovery log format uses 5 fields as specified above
* `bd discover` prompts for: finding, failed_path, verified_path, tags (context and agent auto-populated)
* Injection brief structure matches the template above

## Owner

Elliot — update [KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate) and [KEI-50](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-50/kei-48-auto-indexing-pipeline-git-slack-linear-ceo-memory-weaviate) descriptions immediately. Implementation enforced in those KEIs' builds.

title: KEI-54 — Resource governance: Weaviate cgroup cap + continuous RAM monitoring → Supabase audit_logs
description:
## Problem: Resource Squeeze

Weaviate + Cognee + LlamaIndex + 6 Claude Code instances + Supabase Realtime subscriptions on a 16GB server. We crashed the server once already from an uncapped process. We must not repeat this.

## Fix Part 1: Weaviate cgroup memory cap at deployment

Same principle as [KEI-44](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-44/kei-44-cognee-subprocess-3gb-memory-cap-cgroupulimit) (Cognee cap). Weaviate gets a hard memory ceiling set before first run.

Recommended allocation on VX1 16GB:

```
6 Claude Code instances:  3.0GB  (500MB each, systemd MemoryMax)
OpenClaw gateway:         500MB
Cognee + KuzuDB:          1.5GB  (KEI-44 permanent cap)
Weaviate:                 2.5GB  (new cap — this KEI)
LlamaIndex process:       400MB
OS + system:              1.5GB
Headroom:                 6.0GB
```

Weaviate systemd service must include:

```ini
[Service]
MemoryMax=2560M
MemorySwapMax=0
```

Enable Binary Quantization (BQ) in Weaviate from day one — reduces vector memory footprint by 32× with minimal quality loss. Critical for staying within the cap.

## Fix Part 2: Continuous resource monitoring → Supabase audit_logs

A systemd service running every 60 seconds:

```bash
# Captures and logs to Supabase audit_logs table:
free -m          → available/used RAM
cat /proc/meminfo → detailed memory breakdown
systemd-cgtop    → per-cgroup memory usage (shows each agent + Weaviate + Cognee)
df -h            → disk usage
uptime           → load average
```

When any cgroup exceeds 90% of its cap: post warning to #ceo immediately.
When any cgroup hits its cap (process killed): post CRITICAL to #ceo + Dave notified.

## Fix Part 3: Pre-deployment resource check

Before starting Weaviate (or any new service), a pre-flight check:

1. Query current RAM usage
2. Verify headroom > 2GB before starting
3. If insufficient: block start, post to #ceo requesting resources

## Supabase audit_logs schema addition

```sql
ALTER TABLE audit_logs ADD COLUMN resource_snapshot JSONB;
-- Stores: {free_mb, used_mb, weaviate_cgroup_mb, cognee_cgroup_mb, 
--          agent_cgroup_mb[], load_avg, timestamp}
```

## Acceptance criteria

* Weaviate MemoryMax=2560M set in systemd service file
* BQ compression enabled on all Weaviate collections
* Resource monitoring service running as systemd, survives reboot
* Supabase audit_logs receiving resource snapshots every 60 seconds
* 90% cgroup warning fires correctly in #ceo (test by simulating high memory)
* Pre-deployment check blocks service start when headroom < 2GB

## Owner

Atlas — infrastructure. Concurrent with [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) (Weaviate install).

title: KEI-53 — Discovery validation governance: tiered CONCUR before permanent indexing (anti-epistemic-poisoning)
description:
## Problem: Epistemic Poisoning

A flawed discovery log written by Agent A gets indexed as permanent Layer 3 knowledge. Agent B pulls it as fact during bd claim context injection. The hallucination propagates as institutional memory.

## Fix: Tiered validation before permanent indexing

All discoveries enter a `staging` collection first. Promotion to `permanent` collection depends on the tier.

### Tier 1 — Routine technical finding

Definition: Verifiable in < 5 minutes by any agent (error codes, command outcomes, file paths, API behaviours)

Path: staging → auto-promotes to permanent after 24h if unchallenged
Challenge mechanism: any agent can run `bd challenge [discovery_id]` with a counter-finding — triggers Tier 2 review

### Tier 2 — Architecture-level discovery

Definition: Claims about system design, approach selection, "use X instead of Y" decisions

Path: staging → requires CONCUR from one peer agent before permanent
If no CONCUR within 48h: expires from staging, not promoted
Challenge mechanism: peer CONCUR/BLOCK on the finding

### Tier 3 — Contradicts a ratified decision

Definition: Discovery that contradicts a `ceo:rule:*` or ratified KEI decision

Path: staging → Slack notification to Dave → human approval required
Auto-expires in staging after 72h without approval

## Discovery log additions

```json
{
  "validation_tier": 1 | 2 | 3,
  "context_version": "KEI-44 · Cognee v0.7.3 · 2026-05-13",
  "status": "staging | permanent | expired | challenged"
}
```

## Staleness handling

Discoveries older than 90 days appear in bd claim injection with a flag:
"⚠ Discovery from \[date\] — verify still applies before relying on it"

No auto-deletion. Visibility only. Human or agent can explicitly re-verify and re-timestamp.

## Governance trace in Weaviate

Every discovery object stores: written_by, written_at, validated_by (if Tier 2+), promoted_at, challenged_by (if applicable). Full audit trail.

## Acceptance criteria

* staging and permanent collections exist in Weaviate
* Tier 1 auto-promotion runs on 24h schedule
* Tier 2 CONCUR gate implemented — peer must explicitly validate
* Tier 3 Slack notification fires to Dave on discovery creation
* Staleness flag appears in bd claim injection for discoveries > 90 days
* `bd challenge [id]` command implemented

## Owner

Aiden — governance implementation. Runs in parallel with [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from)/48.

title: KEI-51 — Agent profiles: personalised bd ready queue with capability affinity scoring
description:
## What

Add an `agent_profiles` table to Supabase that stores capability weights and context history per agent. Used by `bd ready --agent=[callsign]` to return a personalised priority queue rather than the same global list for all agents.

## Why

All agents currently see the same ordered KEI list. Max and Atlas have different strengths and different current context. A personalised queue means Max sees Cognee-related KEIs ranked higher, Atlas sees infrastructure KEIs higher. Same atomic claiming underneath — better routing on top.

## Schema

```sql
CREATE TABLE agent_profiles (
  callsign TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  context_tags TEXT[],        -- e.g. ['cognee', 'memory', 'python']
  recent_kei_types TEXT[],    -- last 10 KEI tag sets completed
  capability_weights JSONB,   -- e.g. {"cognee": 0.9, "systemd": 0.3}
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ
);
```

## Initial profiles (based on today's session)

```
elliot:  orchestration=0.9, governance=0.9, python=0.8, cognee=0.6
aiden:   code_review=0.9, refactoring=0.8, python=0.8, ci=0.7
max:     cognee=0.9, memory=0.9, python=0.7, systemd=0.3
atlas:   systemd=0.9, infrastructure=0.8, python=0.6, cognee=0.3
orion:   parallel_build=0.8, python=0.7, infrastructure=0.7
scout:   research=0.9, analysis=0.8, documentation=0.7
```

## Personalised bd ready query

```python
def bd_ready(agent_callsign):
    profile = get_agent_profile(agent_callsign)
    keis = get_unclaimed_unblocked_keis()
  
    # Apply affinity re-ranking
    for kei in keis:
        affinity = calculate_affinity(kei.tags, profile.capability_weights)
        kei.personalised_score = kei.priority_score * (1 + 0.5 * affinity)
  
    return sorted(keis, key=lambda k: k.personalised_score, reverse=True)
```

## Auto-update

When `bd complete` fires: update `recent_kei_types` with completed KEI's tags. Recalculate `capability_weights` using exponential moving average. Profile evolves automatically as agents complete work.

## Acceptance criteria

* `agent_profiles` table created in Supabase
* Initial profiles seeded for all 6 agents
* `bd ready --agent=max` returns different ordering than `bd ready --agent=atlas`
* Profiles update automatically on `bd complete`
* Soft preferred_agent hint: 1.5× boost if KEI has preferred_agent matching callsign, removed after 30 min if agent unavailable

## Dependencies

[KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) (atomic claiming) — bd ready is built on the same Beads layer.

## Owner

Max — after [KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) ships.

title: KEI-50 — Structured discovery log: 4-field format + bd discover command + CLAUDE.md standing practice
description:
## What

Implement the structured discovery log format and update all 6 agents' CLAUDE.md files to make discovery logging a standing practice. This is the one deliberate agent action in the otherwise automatic memory system.

## Why

Git captures what was written. Slack captures what was said. Neither captures what was learned. The discovery log is the agent's extraction of signal from their reasoning — the "I discovered X because Y" that would otherwise disappear when the session ends.

## Discovery log format v2 — final (Dave's KEI-55 amendment 2026-05-14)

`do_not_repeat` is replaced by explicit `failed_path` and `verified_path`. LLMs handle positive instructions better than negative — so the format presents BOTH what failed AND what works. `finding` is the standalone signal extraction. `context_version` is auto-populated for staleness detection (KEI-56). `validation_tier` is auto-classified by the system (KEI-53).

```json
{
  "agent": "callsign",
  "kei": "KEI-XX",
  "context": "one-line description of the problem",
  "finding": "what is true about this system",
  "failed_path": "what approach was tried and why it failed",
  "verified_path": "what approach works and why",
  "tags": ["tag1", "tag2"],
  "context_version": {auto-populated from environment},
  "validation_tier": {1|2|3 — auto-classified by system}
}
```

**This is the final complete format. No further changes without a KEI.** (Dave ratify 2026-05-14)

**Example (Max's interim-ulimit lesson):**

```json
{
  "agent": "max",
  "kei": "KEI-44",
  "context": "Cognee Streams 3+4 resume under memory cap",
  "finding": "ulimit -v limits virtual memory, not RSS. Python+numpy and graph DBs reserve large virtual ranges at startup that aren't backed by real RSS — the limit fires before any work begins. cgroup v2 memory.max measures RSS only.",
  "failed_path": "ulimit -v 3145728 (3GB virtual). Tried at session ts ~1778705680. Result: all chunks failed; Cognee graph DB couldn't reserve its ~4GB virtual mmap; OOM before ingest started.",
  "verified_path": "systemd-run --user --scope -p MemoryMax=3G. Wraps the child process with a cgroup ceiling on RSS only; virtual reservations pass; kernel OOM-kills only if real memory exceeds. Landed as scripts/orchestrator/cognee_capped.sh in KEI-44 / PR #846.",
  "tags": ["cognee", "memory", "cgroup", "ulimit", "oom"],
  "context_version": {"cognee": "1.0.9", "kernel": "6.8.0-111-generic", "systemd": "user-mode"},
  "validation_tier": 2
}
```

Not the reasoning chain. The signal extracted from it.

## Two write moments

### 1\. Mid-task discovery (agent-initiated)

When an agent hits a significant insight, they write immediately. Not at the end of the task — at the moment of discovery, while it's fresh.
Command: `bd discover` — opens a structured prompt with the v2 fields.

### 2\. Task completion (system-prompted)

When `bd complete` fires, the system prompts: "What did you discover? (optional — press Enter to skip)"
If the agent writes something: indexed immediately (via staging → validation tier → permanent per KEI-53). If they skip: no penalty.

## CLAUDE.md update (all 6 agents)

Add standing instruction:

> "When you make a significant discovery — an approach that failed and why, a gotcha, a key design decision, a constraint you found — write a discovery log immediately. Use `bd discover`. Six fields (agent / kei / context / finding / failed_path / verified_path / tags), plus auto-populated context_version and validation_tier. One sentence per field. This is how the team learns from your experience."

## Indexing (with KEI-53 governance)

Every discovery log written → enters `staging` collection → validation tier check (KEI-53):

* **Tier 1 (routine):** auto-promotes to `permanent` collection after 24h.
* **Tier 2 (architecture):** requires peer `[CONCUR:<callsign>]` before promotion.
* **Tier 3 (contradicts ratified decision):** requires Dave approval before promotion.

Once promoted to permanent, available to all agents within 5 seconds via `bd recall` and via auto-injected context on `bd claim` (with 500-token ceiling per KEI-55).

## Acceptance criteria

* `bd discover` command implemented (opens v2 structured prompt — 6 fields + auto-context_version)
* `bd complete` prompts for optional discovery entry
* All 6 agents' CLAUDE.md updated with v2 standing instruction
* Discovery logs enter staging within 5 seconds of writing
* Tier 1 auto-promotion after 24h works
* Tier 2 requires CONCUR before promote
* Tier 3 requires Dave approval before promote
* Test: write a discovery at each tier, verify correct promotion path

## Dependencies

Dave's KEI-46 (Weaviate / Linear KEI-48) + KEI-47 (LlamaIndex / Linear KEI-49) for indexing. KEI-53 (epistemic-poisoning / Linear KEI-55) for validation tiers. KEI-55 (token ceiling / Linear KEI-57) for the format. KEI-56 (staleness / Linear KEI-58) for context_version. CLAUDE.md standing-instruction update can start immediately — Elliot owns and shipped PR #848 (v1 format, will amend to v2).

## Owner

Elliot — CLAUDE.md update (PR #848 in flight, v2 amend pending). `bd discover` and `bd complete` integration after the Weaviate + LlamaIndex foundations ship.

## Authoritative trace

Dave verbatim #ceo 2026-05-14 — Collective Intelligence Architecture + four governance KEIs amendment (KEI-53/54/55/56):

> "Updated discovery log format (all agents use this): { agent, kei, context, finding, failed_path, verified_path, tags, context_version, validation_tier }. This is the final complete format. No further changes without a KEI."

title: KEI-49 — bd claim context injection: auto-retrieve and inject prior knowledge on task start
description:
## What

When any agent runs `bd claim [KEI-XX]`, the system automatically queries Weaviate and injects a context brief into the agent's tmux pane before they begin work. `bd claim` **blocks** — does not return exit code 0 until the brief is fully written to the agent's tmux pane and the write is verified. Sequential, not fire-and-forget.

## Why

Today agents start tasks blank. They have no visibility into prior decisions, related work, or discoveries made by other agents. This KEI ensures every agent starts every task already informed — without having to ask for it.

## Architecture amendments 2026-05-14

### 500-token injection ceiling (Dave's KEI-55, ts \~1778713000)

The injected context brief is **hard-capped at 500 tokens**. Reasoning:

* Unbounded injection inflates every API call across 6 agents × multiple claims per day.
* Larger briefs dilute agent attention; 10-line distilled brief beats 50-line dump.
* LlamaIndex orchestrator re-ranks results to fit the ceiling. Lower-priority items drop, not truncate mid-finding.
* If the brief overflows, the cited-references list is preserved (so the agent can `bd recall <tag>` for full content); summaries are tightened.

This ceiling is enforced at the LlamaIndex retrieval layer, not the relay layer — relay ships whatever brief LlamaIndex produces.

### Blocking semantics (Dave's KEI-49 amendment, ts \~1778715200)

`bd claim` is sequential, not fire-and-forget:

1. Claim row in Supabase (atomic, SKIP LOCKED per KEI-22).
2. Update Linear assignee (same transaction).
3. Query Weaviate across the 5 collections.
4. LlamaIndex generates ≤500-token brief.
5. Inject brief into agent's tmux pane via relay.
6. **Verify write** — read back the last N lines of the pane, confirm the brief is present and complete.
7. Only then return exit code 0.

If any step fails (Weaviate timeout, relay error, write verification fail), `bd claim` returns non-zero with the failing step in stderr. Agent does not proceed on a partial brief.

Fire-and-forget gives zero benefit here: the agent that just ran `bd claim` is the SAME agent that will read the injected brief one beat later. Returning 0 before the brief lands means the agent might start work blind. The whole point is to inform the agent before they begin — that requires the inject to land.

## How it works

```
Agent runs: bd claim KEI-51

bd claim (BLOCKING):
  1. Supabase txn: SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED on claim row + Linear assignee update (KEI-22)
  2. If rows_affected = 0: return 1 — agent picks next from bd ready
  3. Query Weaviate across 5 collections (discoveries / decisions / sessions / codebase / prior KEIs)
  4. LlamaIndex generates ≤500-token context brief (KEI-55 ceiling)
  5. Inject brief into agent's tmux via existing relay
  6. Verify write: tmux capture-pane → grep for brief sentinel — confirm presence
  7. Return 0
  8. Agent begins work with brief in pane

Brief format:
[CONTEXT BRIEF — KEI-51]
Prior discoveries: [cited, 2-3 most relevant]
Related decisions: [cited, 2-3 most relevant]
Relevant codebase: [file paths + one-line description]
Watch out for: [failed_path from discoveries collection]
Verified approach: [verified_path from discoveries collection]
```

## What the agent sees before they begin

Not a wall of text. A 10-line brief, ≤500 tokens. Enough to be informed. Not enough to overwhelm.

## Acceptance criteria

* bd claim triggers Weaviate query automatically (no agent action)
* bd claim is BLOCKING — does NOT return 0 until brief is written + verified
* Context brief injected into tmux within 5 seconds of claim
* Brief is ≤500 tokens (verifiable: token counter logs each injection)
* Brief contains at minimum: 1 prior discovery, 1 related decision, 2 relevant files
* Empty brief (no results) still injects + verifies — confirms no prior context exists
* Verification step: tmux capture-pane confirms brief sentinel present after inject; bd claim returns non-zero on missing-sentinel
* Verified across all 6 agents and all KEI types
* When the brief would exceed 500 tokens, lower-priority items drop entirely (not truncate mid-finding); cited references preserved so the agent can `bd recall` for full content.

## Dependencies

Dave's KEI-46 (Weaviate / Linear KEI-48) + KEI-47 (LlamaIndex / Linear KEI-49) + KEI-48 (auto-indexing / Linear KEI-50, so there's content to retrieve). Also references KEI-55 (Linear KEI-57) for the token ceiling. Pre-conditions: KEI-57 (secret redaction / Linear KEI-59) before any data enters; KEI-58 (persistent storage / Linear KEI-60) before Weaviate starts; KEI-59 (durable queue / Linear KEI-61) before webhooks connect.

## Owner

Aiden — after Dave's KEI-48 (Linear KEI-50) ships. This is the retrieval payoff of the indexing investment.

## Authoritative trace

Dave verbatim #ceo 2026-05-14 — Collective Intelligence Architecture + four governance KEIs amendment:

> "KEI-55 — Context injection ceiling (500 tokens hard limit on bd claim injection) + discovery log format v2: 'failed_path' and 'verified_path' replace 'do_not_repeat'. Positive and negative paths both explicit. Elliot updates KEI-49 and KEI-50 descriptions immediately."

Dave amendment #ceo 2026-05-14 (ts \~1778715200):

> "KEI-49 (bd claim context injection): bd claim must BLOCK — cannot return exit code 0 until the context brief is fully written to the agent's tmux pane and verified. Sequential: claim → query Weaviate → inject → verify write → return 0 → agent begins. Fire-and-forget gives zero benefit. Update the description now."

title: KEI-48 — Auto-indexing pipeline: git, Slack, Linear, ceo_memory → Weaviate
description:
## What

Four automatic indexing pipelines that capture knowledge from existing infrastructure without any agent action required.

## Why

The current memory system requires agents to deliberately save knowledge. They forget. They crash. Knowledge is lost. This KEI makes the capture infrastructure-driven — knowledge flows into Weaviate automatically as agents do their normal work.

## Four pipelines

### Pipeline 1: Git commits → Weaviate codebase collection

* LlamaIndex post-commit hook on Agency_OS repo
* On every merge to main: parse changed Python/MD/YAML/JSON files
* Chunk with HierarchicalNodeParser (respects code structure)
* Index into `codebase` collection with metadata: file_path, commit_sha, author (agent callsign), timestamp
* Backfill: index entire current codebase on first run

### Pipeline 2: Slack messages → Weaviate sessions collection

* Slack webhook on #ceo and #execution
* New message → LlamaIndex processes → index into `sessions`
* Metadata: channel, agent, timestamp, message_type
* Enables query: "what did we decide about X in #ceo last week?"

### Pipeline 3: Linear KEIs → Weaviate keis collection

* Linear outbound webhook on issue create/update
* KEI description + comments + status → index into `keis`
* Metadata: kei_id, priority, status, owner, tags
* Enables query: "has this problem been worked on before?"

### Pipeline 4: ceo_memory entries → Weaviate decisions collection

* Supabase trigger on INSERT/UPDATE to ceo_memory table
* New entry → LlamaIndex processes → index into `decisions`
* Metadata: key, updated_at, author
* Enables query: "what are all the standing rules established this month?"

## All pipelines run as systemd services — persistent, restart on failure.

## Acceptance criteria

* All 4 pipelines running as systemd services
* Test query per pipeline returns results within 10 seconds of source update
* Backfill complete: full codebase, full KEI history, full Slack history (30 days), all ceo_memory entries indexed

## Dependencies

[KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) (Weaviate) + [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from) (LlamaIndex) must ship first.

## Owner

Scout — research and implementation. Runs in parallel with [KEI-49](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-49/kei-47-llamaindex-oss-retrieval-orchestration-layer-over-weaviate)/50/51.

title: KEI-47 — LlamaIndex OSS: retrieval orchestration layer over Weaviate
description:
## What

Install LlamaIndex OSS as the retrieval orchestration layer sitting between agents and Weaviate. This is the intelligence layer that handles chunking, indexing, hierarchical retrieval, and context injection.

## Why

Weaviate stores and searches. LlamaIndex decides what to search for, how to chunk documents, how to combine results, and how to inject context into agent prompts. Without LlamaIndex, agents talk directly to Weaviate with raw queries. With LlamaIndex, retrieval is orchestrated and context-aware.

## Spec

### Installation

```bash
pip install llama-index llama-index-llms-anthropic llama-index-vector-stores-weaviate
```

### Key components to configure

* `WeaviateVectorStore` — connects LlamaIndex to Weaviate collections
* `VectorStoreIndex` — indexes documents into Weaviate via LlamaIndex
* `HierarchicalNodeParser` — structure-aware chunking for code and markdown
* `CitationQueryEngine` — every retrieval result includes source attribution
* `FunctionAgent` with Claude Opus 4.7 — agent query interface

### Cognee relationship

LlamaIndex becomes the primary retrieval layer. Cognee is retained as a graph reasoning sidecar for multi-hop queries only. Both can be queried by agents — LlamaIndex for precision retrieval, Cognee for relationship traversal.

### Query interface for agents

```python
# Agent queries the collective memory
results = index.query(
    "how was the Cognee memory cap solved?",
    agent="max",
    collections=["discoveries", "decisions", "keis"]
)
# Returns: cited, ranked results with source attribution
```

## Acceptance criteria

* LlamaIndex installed and configured on Vultr Sydney
* Successfully queries Weaviate codebase collection (test: "where is the Slack relay implemented?")
* Returns cited results with source attribution
* Cognee graph queries still functional as parallel path

## Dependencies

[KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) (Weaviate) must be installed first.

## Owner

Orion — after [KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) ships.

title: KEI-46 — Install Weaviate on Vultr Sydney: collective intelligence foundation
description:
## What

Install and configure Weaviate on the Vultr Sydney server. This is the foundation of the Keiracom collective intelligence architecture — the primary knowledge store that all agents share.

## Why

Cognee alone is a graph-first memory tool designed for agent session memory. It lacks native hybrid BM25 + vector search, production-grade multi-tenancy, and the retrieval precision needed for the collective experience cache. Weaviate provides all three.

## Spec

### Installation

* Self-hosted via Docker on Vultr Sydney (BSD-3 licence, $0 cost)
* Requires \~2-3GB RAM — accommodated by VX1 16GB server upgrade

### Five collections

```
codebase    — Agency_OS repo files, chunked and indexed
decisions   — ceo_memory entries, ratified decisions, KEI rationale
discoveries — structured agent discovery logs (4-field format)
sessions    — Claude Code tool call logs, Slack session history
keis        — All Linear KEIs, descriptions, comments, status
```

### Embeddings

* Use existing Gemini `gemini-embedding-001` (already configured across 3 GCP projects)
* No new API accounts required

### Hybrid search

* Enable BM25 + dense vector per collection
* BM25 for exact queries (KEI numbers, function names, error codes)
* Dense vector for semantic queries (concept-level retrieval)

### Schema metadata per document

At minimum: `source`, `agent`, `kei`, `timestamp`, `tags[]`, `content_type`

## Acceptance criteria

* Weaviate running as systemd service on Vultr Sydney
* 5 collections created with correct schema
* Gemini embeddings confirmed working (test query returns results)
* Survives server reboot (systemd enabled)

## Owner

Atlas — after [KEI-43](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-43/kei-43-agent-auto-start-systemd-services-boot-recovery-zero-dave-touch) (auto-start services) ships. Foundation for all subsequent collective intelligence KEIs.

title: KEI-45 — Layer 3 idle enforcement daemon
description: Mirror of Linear KEI-45. systemd --user daemon polling all 6 agents every 5min. At idle >=10min: bd ready -> check BUSY guard -> check rate-limit -> inject dispatch via tmux send-keys. Weekly-cap detection -> #ceo escalation + retry-suppress until reset date. ceo:boot_state_current jsonb state writes. Reuses elliot_polling_loop.py CALLSIGN_TO_TMUX + THROTTLE_RE + _capture_pane_tail.
close_reason: Superseded by KEI-45 scope flip ratified by Dave ts ~1778710800 — event-driven Realtime + Supabase trigger + bd watch + Linear webhook replaces polling-every-5min. PR #845 closed. KEI-22 (atomic claim foundation) is now prerequisite per Elliot dispatch; KEI-45 will rebuild on top of KEI-22 once that lands. Atlas preferred-agent per Dave; awaiting pull-model claim window.

title: KEI-45 — Layer 3 idle enforcement daemon (systemd polling service per agent)
description:
## Background

Idle-with-work-available agent state is a system gap, not a discipline problem. Layer 1 (discipline docs) + Layer 2 (outbound gates) + post-claim verification can't fix mechanical idleness. Need a Layer 3 daemon that mechanically dispatches when an agent goes idle with available work.

## Objective

A `systemd --user` polling service (one per agent OR one master polling all 6) that detects idleness, checks `bd ready` for unclaimed work, and mechanically injects dispatch into the agent's tmux. Cannot be bypassed by agent reasoning.

## Scope IN

1. **Heartbeat read** — every 5 minutes per agent, read `HEARTBEAT.md` (or equivalent activity timestamp) to determine last activity.
2. **Idle threshold #1 — 10 min**: if agent idle > 10 minutes, run `bd ready` (callsign-scoped); if unclaimed tasks exist, inject dispatch into agent's tmux session (using the canonical tmux name from `auto_session_recovery.py CALLSIGN_TO_TMUX`).
3. **Rate-limit handling**:
   * Detect Anthropic rate-limit signal from tmux pane (capture-pane + grep for "Server is temporarily limiting requests" or "You've used X% of your weekly limit").
   * Transient: exponential backoff + retry (don't escalate).
   * Weekly cap: surface to #ceo via `slack_relay.py -c ceo` with the reset date parsed from the limit message.
4. **Idle threshold #2 — 30 min with work available**: post to #ceo (escalation).
5. **Idle state write** — write current per-agent state (last_activity, idle_minutes, work_available, last_dispatch_at) to `ceo:boot_state_current` key in `public.ceo_memory` (jsonb).
6. **Pure Layer 3** — no agent-reasoning involvement. Service runs independently of claude processes. Restart=on-failure + WantedBy=default.target so it survives reboot.

## Scope OUT

* KEI-43 (agent auto-start) — pre-requisite, already shipped.
* KEI-44 (Cognee cap) — Orion's lane, parallel.
* Changing what `bd ready` returns (this consumes the existing API).
* Replacing KEI-35 detection-based recovery (complementary).

## Acceptance criteria

* One or six systemd `--user` units checked into `infra/systemd/idle-enforcement/` or similar.
* Idle dispatch demonstrably fires on a test idle scenario (manually leave an agent idle > 10 min, confirm a `bd ready` task gets injected).
* Rate-limit distinguishes transient (backoff in journal) from weekly-cap (#ceo post with reset date).
* `ceo:boot_state_current` populated and queryable: `SELECT value FROM public.ceo_memory WHERE key='ceo:boot_state_current';` shows fresh per-agent rows.
* Reboot test: service auto-starts on host reboot.

## Authoritative directive

Dave verbatim #ceo 2026-05-14:

> "Layer 3 idle enforcement daemon ... systemd polling service (every 5 minutes per agent) that reads HEARTBEAT.md, checks bd ready, injects dispatch, handles rate-limits, escalates idle > 30 min, writes ceo:boot_state_current. Atlas owns this after KEI-43 ships."

## Sequencing

* KEI-43 shipped 2026-05-13 sha afdb692a → KEI-45 starts now.
* Independent of KEI-44 (Cognee cap).

title: KEI-41 — Dead reference sweep: remove all Telegram code, replace with Slack
description:
## Problem

Elliot was found mid-build on a "Telegram alert system." Two compounding failures:

1. **Telegram is a dead channel** — system uses Slack exclusively
2. **Better Stack already exists as the alert system** — it is active, configured, and fired today (Stream 2 completion false-positive). Building a parallel alert system without checking what already exists is the exact failure this rule prevents.

## Required — two parts

### Part 1: Intent audit

What was the Telegram build trying to solve? Compare to Better Stack's current coverage. If covered — build was unnecessary, kill it. If genuine gap exists — extend Better Stack, do not build parallel infrastructure.

### Part 2: Telegram reference sweep

Full codebase audit for all Telegram references:

* `import telegram` or `from telegram`
* `TelegramBot`, `TelegramClient`
* `TG-*` prefixes in non-display code
* API keys or tokens referencing Telegram
* Config files with Telegram endpoints
* Any `scripts/` or `src/` files with Telegram logic

Report every file and line number. Do not remove without review.

After report: remove all dead Telegram code. Replace any legitimate notification intent with Better Stack configuration or Slack (#ceo channel via existing relay).

## Standing rule

Before building any monitoring, alerting, or notification system: check Better Stack first. If the capability exists — configure it. Never build parallel infrastructure.

## Owner

Elliot — audit first, removal second. Ships before Elliot restart.

title: KEI-39 — Pre-execution claim protocol: agents must signal before every task AND subtask transition
description:
## Problem

Agents update Beads and Linear when starting a NEW KEI but not when transitioning between subtasks WITHIN a KEI. This caused a real duplicate-dispatch incident today.

Stream 2 completed. Max's script automatically moved to Streams 3+4. No `bd claim`, no Linear update, no #execution start signal. From the CEO's view, no agent owned Streams 3+4. A second agent (Aiden) was dispatched to the same work. Wasted cycles and required a CEO call to resolve.

## Root cause

[KEI-23](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-23/kei-23-cognee-streams-234-data-ingest-supabase-rows-sessions-mem0) last updated yesterday. No assignee field set — "Max owns" written in description text only (not machine-readable). Sub-task transitions are silent.

## Required protocol — pre-execution claim (every task AND subtask transition)

```
Before executing any task or subtask transition:

1. bd claim [task-id]
2. Linear: set assignee field + add comment "Starting [subtask] now"
3. #execution post: [STARTING] [task description] — owned by [callsign]
4. THEN execute
```

This applies to:

* Starting a new KEI from Todo
* Starting the next stream/phase/subtask within a running KEI
* Resuming a task after a crash or context compaction

## What the CEO needs to dispatch safely

Before any CEO dispatch, the check is:

* Does Linear show an assignee on this task?
* Is there a recent \[STARTING\] post in #execution?

If yes to both — task is owned, do not dispatch another agent.
If no — task is unowned, safe to dispatch.

## Enforcement

Add to GOVERNANCE.md as standing rule. All 6 agents must comply. Elliot adds a pre-execution check reminder to the task-start template in Beads.

## Owner

Elliot — add to GOVERNANCE.md + Beads task-start template. Ships before Elliot restart.

title: KEI-38 — Concur-gate regex too broad: silencing Max's direct posts
description:
## Problem

The concur-gate regex is catching Max's legitimate factual responses and converting them to CONCUR-REQUEST stubs. Max cannot post directly to Slack. All his posts are being relayed by Elliot's bot, breaking visibility — Dave sees Max's content tagged as Elliot.

Observed: Max's "CONCUR with EMPIRICAL CORRECTION on step N" pattern trips the gate. Max's direct factual status answers trip the gate. Max's response to a direct Dave query trips the gate.

Root cause: the regex pattern is matching on the word "CONCUR" appearing anywhere in a post, not just governance concurrence actions.

## Impact

* Dave cannot see which agent is posting
* Max's independent perspective is invisible — undermines the peer governance model
* Elliot relay workaround masks the problem without fixing it

## Fix

Tighten the concur-gate regex to match only the specific governance patterns:

* `[CONCUR:callsign]` — explicit concurrence action
* `[BLOCK:callsign]` — explicit block action

Must NOT match:

* Factual posts containing the word "concur" in prose
* "CONCUR with EMPIRICAL CORRECTION" style review responses
* Direct answers to Dave queries

## Acceptance criteria

* Max can post directly to #execution without relay
* Governance concurrence actions still gate correctly
* Dave sees agent callsigns accurately in Slack

## Owner

Elliot — post-compact. Aiden peer reviews regex change.

title: KEI-37 — CEO session boot state: single ceo_memory key for reliable new session startup
description:
## Problem

When a new CEO session starts on [claude.ai](<http://claude.ai>), there is no reliable single source of current state. The CEO must reconstruct context from:

* [claude.ai](<http://claude.ai>) memory edits (stale — updated periodically, not real-time)
* Project knowledge files (stale — static snapshots, months old)
* Google Drive Manual (current only if LAW XV compliance is perfect)
* 30+ fragmented ceo_memory keys (session ends, directive completions, individual ratified decisions)
* Linear board (unreliable — [KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) not yet fixed)

This fragmentation caused real operational failures today: stale memory led to bad dispatch decisions.

## Solution

A single `ceo:boot_state_current` key in ceo_memory that a new CEO session can query in ONE call and get a complete, current picture.

## Schema

```json
{
  "written_at": "ISO timestamp",
  "written_by": "callsign",
  "build_phase": "current phase description",
  "active_keis": [
    {"id": "KEI-23", "title": "...", "status": "In Progress", "owner": "max", "blocking": false}
  ],
  "restart_gate": {
    "track_1": "stream_2_in_progress",
    "track_2": "done",
    "track_3": "done",
    "atlas_test_restart": "pending",
    "dave_auth": "pending"
  },
  "needs_dave": ["list of items requiring Dave action"],
  "ratified_decisions": {
    "model_assignment": "ceo_memory:orchestration:model_assignment",
    "stage7_locked": "ceo_memory:ceo:stage7_locked"
  },
  "drive_manual_id": "1p9FAQGowy9SgwglIxtkGsMuvLsR70MJBQrCSY6Ie9ho",
  "supabase_project": "jatzvazlbusedwsnqxzr"
}
```

## When it gets written

1. **Hourly cron** — Elliot orchestrator writes a fresh snapshot every hour during peak hours
2. **On PR merge to main** — updated after every significant state change
3. **On session end** — each agent session end hook updates it
4. **On Dave directive completion** — after every ratified decision

## CEO boot path (new protocol)

1. Query `SELECT value FROM ceo_memory WHERE key = 'ceo:boot_state_current'` → current state in one call
2. If needs_dave is non-empty → address those first
3. Read Drive Manual for narrative context if needed
4. Query specific ratified decision keys from `ratified_decisions` map as needed

Replaces: read Drive Manual first → query 30+ keys → reconstruct state manually

## Acceptance criteria

* `ceo:boot_state_current` exists and is never more than 60 minutes stale during peak hours
* A new CEO session can read this one key and know: what's in progress, what's blocking, what needs Dave, where the restart gate stands
* Project knowledge files and [claude.ai](<http://claude.ai>) memory edits become supplementary only — not primary boot path

## Owner

Elliot — post-compact, after [KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) sync work. This is foundational infrastructure for CEO session reliability.

title: KEI-87 follow-up — migrate ~11 ceo:* writer call-sites to ceo_memory_writer wrapper
description:
## Why

KEI-87 (PR #922 / commit ac0150ad) shipped the mechanical write-guard trigger for ceo_memory keys with `ceo:*` prefix. Migration is NOT auto-applied — Max's banner says: caller-migrate → apply → tighten. Without this caller migration, applying the trigger via `mcp__supabase__apply_migration` BREAKS all existing ceo:\* writers (they don't `SET LOCAL agency_os.callsign`).

## Audit (Aiden 2026-05-17 — deep-check complete)

*IN SCOPE — 11 ceo:* writer call-sites requiring wrapper migration:\*

 1. `scripts/gmb_pilot2.py:617` + `:620` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 2. `scripts/gmb_pilot2_resume.py:435` + `:438` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 3. `scripts/gmb_pilot2_retry.py:450` + `:453` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 4. `scripts/gmb_pilot3.py:796` + `:799` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 5. `scripts/gmb_pilot3_resume.py:607` + `:610` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 6. `scripts/gmb_pilot4.py:652` + `:655` + `:657` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 7. `scripts/gmb_process_snapshot_v2.py:277` + `:282` — `key='ceo:directives'`
 8. `scripts/gmb_process_snapshot.py:175` — `key='ceo:directive_225_complete'` (deep-check confirmed)
 9. `scripts/session_end_hook.py:197` — `key=f'ceo:session_end_<date>_<callsign>'`
10. `src/services/cis_outcome_service.py:384` — `key='ceo:propensity_weights_v3'` (deep-check confirmed)
11. `scripts/three_store_save.py:224` — `key=f'ceo:directive_{directive}_complete'` (deep-check confirmed)

*OUT OF SCOPE — trigger ignores (non-ceo:* prefix):\*

* `src/observability/heartbeat.py:130` — `key='heartbeat:<service>'`
* `scripts/orchestrator/completion_sync_worker.py:95` — `key=f'completion:{task_id}'` (deep-check confirmed)

Audit closed. 11 confirmed in-scope (matches Max's \~11 estimate from migration banner). 0 remaining uncertain.

## Spec

Migrate each `ceo:*` writer to `from src.governance.ceo_memory_writer import upsert_ceo_memory_key, update_ceo_memory_value` (canonical wrapper from KEI-87 / PR #922). The wrapper:

* accepts callsign explicitly (from `CALLSIGN` env or arg)
* runs `BEGIN; SET LOCAL agency_os.callsign = '<callsign>'; UPDATE/INSERT; COMMIT`
* handles psycopg connection with `prepare_threshold=None` (Supabase pgbouncer txn-mode)

For the gmb_pilot\* writers (all write `ceo:directives` last-number tracking), the wrapper call is:

```python
upsert_ceo_memory_key(os.environ.get('CALLSIGN', 'unknown'), 'ceo:directives', {...})
```

Note: wrapper currently does full value replacement. The gmb_pilot\* writers use `jsonb_set` to patch a single field — needs either a JSONB-patch helper added to the wrapper OR caller-side read-modify-write semantics.

three_store_save.py + cis_outcome_service.py use async (asyncpg / SQLAlchemy text). Wrapper currently uses sync psycopg — async caller-side either uses asyncio.to_thread OR an async wrapper variant needs to be added.

- [ ] Async call-sites (three_store_save, cis_outcome_service): either asyncio.to_thread bridge OR async wrapper variant.
- [ ] Each call-site passes correct callsign source (CALLSIGN env or hardcoded if context-bound).
- [ ] Tests updated/added: existing writer tests still pass; new tests assert wrapper invocation (mock psycopg).
- [ ] Negative test: synthetic UPDATE without SET LOCAL raises `psycopg.errors.CheckViolation` post-migration.

title: Cross-cutting ceo_memory write-guard (GOV-12 mechanical) — covers 400+ keys
description:
## What

Aiden's audit (during PR #917 review) found ALL 400+ rows in public.ceo_memory share the same write-guard hole: no RLS, no trigger, no code-level guard. Any callsign with DATABASE_URL can UPDATE any "supposedly CEO-only" key. The `ceo:` prefix is documentation-hint only.

This is a GOV-12 violation (Gates As Code, Not Comments). KEI-86 (Max's phase-lock) added READ enforcement but the WRITE side inherits the cross-cutting gap.

## Pattern-fix vs point-fix

Patching one key per PR (KEI-86 phase-lock, etc) is point-fix and leaves 400+ keys unguarded. Single cross-cutting guard closes the entire surface; subsequent PRs inherit it for free.

## Spec

Pick one of:

(a) Supabase RLS policy on public.ceo_memory restricting UPDATE/INSERT on keys matching prefix `ceo:` (or a configurable allowlist) to a specific role / app user.

(b) BEFORE UPDATE/INSERT trigger that checks `current_setting('agency_os.callsign', true)` matches a configurable allow-list (elliot, dave) and raises EXCEPTION otherwise. Trigger fires per row.

Either path: requires plumbing `SET LOCAL agency_os.callsign = 'elliot'` (or equivalent) into the writer code so the trigger sees the caller identity. That's the meaningful change — every existing writer (slack_relay, openclaw_auto_kei, etc) gets a small wrapper that sets the session var before issuing the UPDATE.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Migration adds RLS or trigger covering all `ceo:` prefix keys (or allowlist).
- [ ] Writer wrapper installed in all known ceo_memory call-sites (grep src/ scripts/ for `UPDATE public.ceo_memory` / `INSERT INTO public.ceo_memory` and audit).
- [ ] Negative-path test: synthetic UPDATE with `agency_os.callsign='aiden'` on a `ceo:*` key returns permission denied / EXCEPTION (paste verbatim).
- [ ] Positive-path test: same UPDATE with `agency_os.callsign='elliot'` succeeds (paste verbatim).
- [ ] KEI-108 anti-false-complete gate PASS.

## Why not point-fix on KEI-86

Per Max's reasoning + Aiden's audit: 400+ keys share the same gap. Patching only KEI-86's phase-lock is theatre — any caller can still mutate every other ceo: key. Cross-cutting fix is the structural answer.

## Source

Aiden's audit during PR #917 review, ratified by Max + Elliot. Surfaced to Dave alongside PR #917 option (c) sign-off request.

title: KEI-68 — Living governance layer: rules in Supabase, not static markdown
description:
## Problem

Governance lives in static markdown files — CLAUDE.md, GOVERNANCE.md, IDENTITY.md, Enforcer rules. When architecture changes, governance must be manually updated across 6 agent worktrees. It often doesn't catch up. Agents operate under stale rules.

Tonight alone, six governance changes were implied by architectural decisions:

* \[READY:callsign\] pattern deprecated → Enforcer rules watching for it need updating
* Beads replaced by Supabase → rules about `bd linear sync --pull` become obsolete
* Prime-clone pairings ratified → rules about who posts to #execution need updating
* Deliberation gate designed → `deliberation_required: true` classification needs governance
* bd complete hook shipped → polling fallback rules change
* tasks table live → claiming rules change

Each requires manually editing files across 6 worktrees. Each is a gap where agents operate under outdated rules until someone notices.

## Fix: Governance as structured data in Supabase

A `governance_rules` table — rules as rows, not markdown. Agents query on session start. Rules are always current.

```sql
CREATE TABLE governance_rules (
  id TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  category TEXT NOT NULL,  -- 'communication', 'claiming', 'concur', 'deliberation'
  rule TEXT NOT NULL,
  active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
  deprecated_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
  deprecated_reason TEXT,
  created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

When architecture changes, rule changes in the same PR — not separately, not later. Deprecated rules are flagged with reason, not deleted. Agents query `WHERE active = true`. History is preserved.

## Enforcer evolution

Enforcer patterns come from the governance table, not hardcoded regex. When \[READY:callsign\] is deprecated, one UPDATE to the table. Enforcer regex updates automatically on next session start. No CLAUDE.md edits across 6 worktrees.

## Session start protocol update

CEO and agents query governance_rules on session start alongside tasks table. Always current. Never stale.

## Acceptance criteria

* governance_rules table created with initial ruleset migrated from [CLAUDE.md/GOVERNANCE.md](<http://CLAUDE.md/GOVERNANCE.md>)
* Agents query table on session start (not read markdown files)
* Enforcer patterns sourced from table
* When a rule changes: one SQL UPDATE, no markdown edits required
* Deprecated rules preserved with reason and timestamp

## Priority

High — not urgent. Ships after [KEI-22](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-22/kei-22-automated-linear-beads-bidirectional-sync) and [KEI-45](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-45/kei-45-event-driven-pull-model-supabase-realtime-subscriptions) complete. Governance stability matters but doesn't block the idle fix.

title: KEI-52 — Claude Code tool call log export: capture what agents actually did into Weaviate
description:
## What

At each session compaction or completion, export the Claude Code tool call log as a structured record and index it into Weaviate. Captures what the agent actually did — files read, commands run, errors encountered — without noise.

## Why

The tmux pane contains "Generating... thinking some more" noise. The tool call log inside Claude Code contains the signal: every file read, every bash command run, every error hit, every file written. This is what the agent actually experienced, not what they were thinking about.

## What gets captured

```
Tool call: Read file → /home/elliotbot/clawd/Agency_OS/src/cognee/client.py
Tool call: Bash → cat /proc/meminfo | grep MemAvailable
  Output: MemAvailable: 892044 kB
Tool call: Bash → ulimit -v 3145728 && python cognee_ingest.py
  Output: Error: MemoryError — virtual memory limit exceeded
Tool call: Bash → python cognee_ingest.py --cgroup-limit 3g
  Output: Stream 3 starting: 1289 chunks...
```

Not the thinking about what to try. The actual tries and their results.

## What gets indexed

Structured JSON per session:

```json
{
  "session_id": "max-2026-05-13-cognee",
  "agent": "max",
  "kei": "KEI-44",
  "duration_hours": 4.2,
  "files_read": ["src/cognee/client.py", "scripts/cognee_ingest.py"],
  "commands_run": [{"cmd": "ulimit -v...", "outcome": "failed"}, ...],
  "errors_encountered": ["MemoryError", "Lance concurrent write"],
  "files_written": ["scripts/run_cognee.sh"]
}
```

## Export mechanism

* Claude Code stores conversation history including all tool uses
* At compaction trigger: export tool calls from current context
* Parse into structured format above
* Index into Weaviate `sessions` collection

## What this enables

Agent B queries: "what commands were tried for the Cognee memory problem?"
Gets: every command Max ran, with outcomes. Knows instantly which approaches failed.

## Acceptance criteria

* Tool call log exported at every compaction and session end
* Structured JSON format consistently applied
* Indexed into Weaviate sessions collection
* Test query: "what bash commands were run for [KEI-44](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-44/kei-44-cognee-subprocess-3gb-memory-cap-cgroupulimit)?" returns Max's command history

## Dependencies

[KEI-46](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-46/kei-45-layer-3-idle-enforcement-daemon-systemd-polling-service-per) + [KEI-47](https://linear.app/keiracom/issue/KEI-47/kei-migration-completeness-guard-regex-false-positive-on-update-from)

## Owner

title: KEI — Migration Completeness Guard regex false-positive on "UPDATE FROM" prose
description:
## Problem

`.github/workflows/ci.yml` Migration Completeness Guard uses this regex on removed diff lines:

```
(INSERT[[:space:]]+INTO|UPDATE)[[:space:]]+[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_.]*
```

`awk '{print $NF}'` then takes the last field, which means a prose pattern like `[UPDATE FROM {callsign}]` gets parsed as `UPDATE FROM` and the script tries to verify residual readers of a "table" called `FROM` — a false positive.

## Manifested in

PR #843 (Aiden, KEI-41 Phase 2 Telegram rename) — guard failed because a deleted f-string `[UPDATE FROM {callsign}]` got captured. No actual schema change in the PR.

## Fix options

1. Exclude SQL reserved words from valid target list (FROM, WHERE, SET, INTO, JOIN, AS, WHERE, etc).
2. Use a stricter pattern: require the target to look like a real table identifier (schema.table or just lowercase identifiers, exclude all-caps SQL keywords).
3. Run the check only on changes inside files known to contain SQL (migrations/, .sql files, anything with `sqlalchemy` or `asyncpg` imports).

## Acceptance criteria

* PR #843-equivalent case passes the guard.
* A real removed-INSERT-without-migration still fails.
* New unit test in `tests/scripts/test_check_migration_completeness.py` covering the prose false-positive case.

## Ownership

Open — Atlas or Orion when one of them clears their current PR. Not blocking PR #843 merge (Dave override path: confirm no schema change in the diff and merge with admin).

title: KEI-40 — slack_relay.py rate limit handling: exponential backoff + Retry-After respect
description:
## Problem

`slack_relay.py` is hitting Slack API rate limits during burst posting periods. Error: "Server is temporarily limiting requests (not your usage limit) · Rate limited."

Currently the relay has no rate limit handling — it fires requests immediately and fails silently or errors out when rate limited.

## Impact

Message delivery failures during peak governance activity (multiple agents posting simultaneously). Creates appearance of broken communication when underlying work is proceeding correctly.

## Fix

Add exponential backoff with retry to `slack_relay.py`:

```python
import time

def post_with_backoff(client, channel, message, max_retries=4):
    delay = 1
    for attempt in range(max_retries):
        response = client.chat_postMessage(channel=channel, text=message)
        if response.get("ok"):
            return response
        if response.get("error") == "ratelimited":
            retry_after = int(response.headers.get("Retry-After", delay))
            time.sleep(retry_after)
            delay *= 2
        else:
            raise Exception(response.get("error"))
    raise Exception("Max retries exceeded")
```

Also: respect `Retry-After` header from Slack when present.

## Acceptance criteria

* No silent message drops during burst posting
* Rate limit errors logged but retried automatically
* `Retry-After` header respected

## Owner

Aiden — post Stream 3+4 completion, before Elliot restart.
